title: Agents as first-class principals
summary: Register an agent, get its automatic keypairs, and exchange its API key for a short-lived JWT.

blocks.text: In 1Claw an agent is a real principal with its own identity, not a shared human login. An agent can authenticate three ways: an API key with the ocv_ prefix, mutual TLS, or OIDC federation. This lesson uses the API key path because it's the fastest to run end to end.

items: API key: ocv_ random value, argon2-hashed, shown once at creation
mTLS: client certificate identity for infrastructure agents
OIDC: federate an external identity provider into 1Claw

blocks.text: When you register an agent, 1Claw auto-generates an Ed25519 signing keypair and a P-256 ECDH keypair. Private keys live HSM-encrypted in the __agent-keys vault; only the public keys sit on the agent record.

steps.text: Get a human user API key (prefix 1ck_) from the dashboard at Settings then API Keys. Agent keys cannot create other agents, so you need a user key here.
Register an agent. The plaintext ocv_ key is returned once in this response and never again. Set token_ttl_seconds and vault_ids to scope the token.
Save the agent_id and api_key from the response, then exchange them for a JWT. This endpoint needs no bearer token; the credentials in the body are the auth.
Confirm the token works by fetching the agent's own profile. You should see the ssh_public_key and ecdh_public_key fields.

blocks.text: You now have a registered agent and a fresh JWT bound to its vaults and TTL. Every later call in this track uses that Bearer token.

quiz.question: How many times is the plaintext ocv_ API key returned?
options: Once, at agent creation or key rotation
Never, it stays in the HSM
quiz.explanation: Only the argon2 hash is stored; the plaintext is shown once at creation or rotation.

quiz.question: Which endpoint turns an agent_id plus api_key into a JWT?
quiz.explanation: POST /v1/auth/agent-token takes the credentials in the body and returns a short-lived access_token.

quiz.question: What two keypairs does 1Claw auto-generate for a new agent?
quiz.explanation: Every agent gets an Ed25519 signing keypair and a P-256 ECDH keypair at creation.

title: Policies and scoped access
summary: Grant an agent read access to exactly one secret path with a glob policy.

blocks.text: A fresh agent has zero access. Its JWT carries an empty scope list until a human writes a policy. Policies tie a principal to path patterns and permissions, so you decide exactly what an agent can touch.

items: ** matches zero or more segments (any depth)
read: GET a value and list metadata
write: PUT and DELETE at matching paths

blocks.text: When you create, update, or delete an agent's policy, all of that agent's active JWTs are revoked immediately. The agent must re-exchange its API key to pick up the new scope.

steps.text: Start with a human user key and the vault and agent UUIDs from the previous lesson.
Create a policy that lets the agent read only api-keys/openai. The pattern is a single path, so the agent cannot reach anything else in the vault.
Re-exchange the agent key for a fresh JWT. The old token was revoked by the policy change.
Read the allowed path (works), then try a different path (returns 403).

blocks.text: Policies also support runtime conditions: an IP allowlist (specific IPs or CIDR ranges) and a time window (allowed hours and days). Add these when an agent runs from known infrastructure.
Your agent can now read one secret path and nothing else, and any policy change takes effect on the very next request.

quiz.question: What access does an agent have before any policy is written?
options: Read on its own vault only
Read plus write on the default vault
quiz.explanation: 1Claw is safe-by-default: no scopes and no policies means the JWT grants nothing.

quiz.question: In a policy pattern, what does ** match?
options: Zero or more segments at any depth
quiz.explanation: * matches one segment while ** matches zero or more segments (any depth).

quiz.question: What happens to active JWTs when an agent's policy changes?
options: They gain the new scope automatically
quiz.explanation: Any policy create, update, or delete revokes the agent's active JWTs so stale scopes cannot linger.

title: Agent self-enrollment and approvals
summary: Let an agent enroll itself with no credentials and route the approval to a human.

blocks.text: Sometimes the agent boots before anyone has provisioned it. The enroll endpoint is public, so an agent can request its own identity and wait for a human to approve. The API key is emailed to the human after approval and never returned to the agent.

steps.text: No credentials are required. Submit an enrollment with a name and an optional human_email. With an email, Allow and Deny links are sent to that address.
steps.code:
curl -s -X POST https://api.1claw.xyz/v1/agents/enroll \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"name":"deploy-bot","human_email":"alice@example.com","description":"CI pipeline agent"}'

steps.text: If you send name only, the response includes an approval_url the human opens while signed in. No email needed to start.
The human approves via the emailed link or the approval_url. After approval an agent is created in their org and the ocv_ key is emailed to them.
The human then writes a policy (see the policies lesson) so the new agent can read the paths it needs.

blocks.text: For sensitive actions an agent can also file an approval with POST /v1/approvals/request, directed to its creator. Requests carry a risk tier: T1 is informational, T2 requires biometric step-up, and T3 requires passkey plus TOTP attestation before the human can approve.
Enrollment is rate limited: one per email per 10 minutes when email is used, plus caps on pending rows and per-IP limits. Some branches return a uniform 201 to limit email enumeration.
You can now bootstrap an agent from zero credentials and hand the approval to a human, who receives the key and grants scoped access.

quiz.question: Where does the enrolled agent's API key go?
options: Emailed to the human after approval
quiz.explanation: The plaintext key is emailed to the approving human and is never returned from the enroll endpoint.

quiz.question: Which risk tier requires passkey plus TOTP attestation?
quiz.explanation: T1 is informational, T2 requires biometric, and T3 requires passkey plus TOTP.

quiz.question: What can an agent submit with name only and no email?
options: A link-only enrollment returning an approval_url
A full agent with an immediate key
quiz.explanation: Name-only enrollment creates a link-only pending enrollment and returns an approval_url for a signed-in human.

title: MCP server integration
summary: Connect an AI client to the 1Claw MCP server and run a first list_secrets call.

blocks.text: The 1Claw MCP server gives an AI client just-in-time access to vault secrets over the Model Context Protocol. The hosted server runs at https://mcp.1claw.xyz/mcp and authenticates per connection with a Bearer JWT and an X-Vault-ID header. The MCP server itself enforces nothing: every call is checked against the agent's policies by the vault API.

steps.text: Mint an agent JWT with your agent_id and ocv_ key. This is the Bearer value the client sends.
Add the hosted server to your client config, for example Claude Desktop at claude_desktop_config.json. Include the vault UUID in X-Vault-ID.
Prefer local stdio? Run the server as a process and pass only the API key. It exchanges the key for a JWT, auto-refreshes, and auto-discovers the vault.
Restart the client and ask it to list your secrets. The agent calls list_secrets and returns paths and metadata, never values.
steps.code: List the secrets in my 1claw vault.

blocks.text: Output inspection runs by default. If a known secret value ever appears in a non-secret tool's output, it is replaced with [REDACTED:#hashprefix] before it reaches the model context.
Your AI client is now wired to the vault, and a first list_secrets call returns metadata scoped to the agent's policies.

quiz.question: Which two headers authenticate a hosted MCP connection?
quiz.explanation: The hosted server takes a Bearer JWT in Authorization and the vault UUID in X-Vault-ID.

quiz.question: Who enforces access control for MCP tool calls?
quiz.explanation: The MCP server just relays requests; the vault API checks every call against the agent's policies.

quiz.question: What does list_secrets return?
options: Paths, types, versions, and metadata only
quiz.explanation: list_secrets returns metadata and never secret values; use get_secret for a value.
